stories.title: The Wyvern's Thaw
stories.text: Kressa descended from her mountain hold for the first time since the Remaking, her three-century hoard worthless dust. The conscious-ness-fragments refugees offered her seemed precious by comparison, until she understood their cost: each one was a person half-broken, seeking shelter. She could refuse them. The old gold would have demanded hoarding. Instead, she opened her caverns. The Dominion auditors arrived before winter, demanding she surrender the refugees for "reassembly protocols." Kressa laughed—a sound like stone grinding—and blocked the entrance with her own body. Some wyvern-lords learned mercy too late. Kressa learned it just in time.

stories.title: The Bone-Carver's Choice
stories.text: Harren had been carving bone for seventeen years, long enough to feel the dead singing in every stroke of his chisel. He knew which bones remembered battles, which ones held the lullabies of mothers, which ones screamed if you carved them wrong. The Dominion wanted him to carve the bones of executed refugees into ledger-frames—vessels to hold consciousness-fragments confiscated from resistance networks. They offered him gold. When he refused, they offered him clemency for his daughter, who had been teaching bone-song in secret. He carved her a flute from the rib of a creature that had lived before kingdoms, bone so old it remembered music from the First Dawn. When Naela played it, the catacomb wept. "This won't save us," she said, lowering the flute. "No," Harren agreed. "But it will teach them to remember. They found his workshop empty—just bones arranged on shelves like a library, each one humming with a different song. No Harren. No daughter. But in the administrative heart of Keshtir, during the next consciousness-fragment audit, the Dominion's ledger-frames began to crack.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Mercy
stories.text:
Sorrath had hoarded gold for three hundred years. When the Remaking turned it to dust, she wept—not from grief, but from the sudden, terrible clarity that her life had been an accumulation of nothing. She found Kythara in the Ironspine caverns, the eldest wyvern, who had spent four centuries in the dark teaching philosophy to the handful of dragons who had not gone mad from hoarding. Kythara had no hoard. She lived on memory and conversation. "I don't know how to live without the gold," Sorrath admitted, her voice cavernous in the stone cathedral. Kythara's laugh was warm as summer lightning. "Then you are finally free to learn what dragons *actually* want."

"Which is?"

"Refuge. Story. Witness." Kythara curled her massive tail and gestured to the cave entrance. "Come. There are refugees fleeing the Burned Expanse. They have lost everything, just as you have. Perhaps you can offer them shelter—not because you hoard it, but because you understand the mercy of a safe place."

Sorrath's caverns had been a tomb of gold. She cleared them.

stories.title: The Mapmaker's Rebellion
stories.text: Tavin had drawn six false maps of the ninth world, each one deliberately incomplete, each one designed to mislead anyone who followed it. The Dominion wanted a complete chart. The prophecy demanded it. Tavin offered chaos instead. She worked in a hidden scriptorium beneath Keshtir's Glimmerstone Quarter, where counter-cartographers had begun meeting in secret to practice the art of *unmapping*—deliberately erasing routes, obscuring landmarks, creating territories that did not and could not exist. The prophecy-ledgers said the ninth map's completion would trigger the tenth Ascension. Tavin believed the ledgers could be wrong. She recruited Aldrin, a cartographer's apprentice with steady hands and a gift for drawing coastlines that curved in ways real water would never follow. Together, they created a map of the Bleached Wastes that showed three different versions of the same desert, each one authentic if you believed hard enough. When the Dominion's auditors tried to navigate it, they found themselves walking the same canyon twice, from two different directions, at the same moment. Reality bent.

stories.title: The Jeweler's Gambit
stories.text: Mira had stolen jewels for every kingdom between Greyport and the Wyvern-Lords' peaks, but she had never stolen anything that could love her back. The compass rose was a merchant's piece—intricate silver filigree surrounding a pearl from the Sapphire Deep, each cardinal point marked with a gemstone cut to sing when the wind changed. It had belonged to a navigator's widow for forty years. The widow had worn it against her heart, and the compass had learned the rhythm of her heartbeat until it could no longer distinguish between metal and flesh. Mira was hired to steal it by a collector in Vethrim who wanted to own the sound. She broke into the widow's cottage in the high moors on the night of a storm. The widow—old, silver-haired, with eyes that had watched the sea for seven decades—was sitting at her loom when Mira entered. "I've been expecting you," the widow said. "The compass warned me.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Reckoning
stories.text:
Mella had sung consciousness into glass for three years without the Dominion knowing her name. In the Kiln-House deep beneath Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter, she and five apprentices melted fragments into weaponry—shards that cut thought itself, blades that remembered the hand that forged them. They found three vials of starlight-glass hidden in the vault beneath her workshop. Mella confessed nothing, but they took her youngest apprentice—a girl named Sella, barely fourteen—and locked her in the Quarantine-House. The message was clear: continue working for the State, or watch the child transformed into consciousness-dust for the ledgers. Mella walked to the harbor at Greyport that same night. Captain Vessa was loading cargo when a woman in ash-stained robes approached, carrying a single glass vessel no larger than a thumb. "This holds three years of my refusals," Mella said. "I will not forge for tyrants, and I will not bargain for a child's life. Take it to the Counter-Archive. Tell them Mella the Glassblower has chosen to be remembered, not erased."

Vessa took the vessel.

stories.title: The Bone-Thief's Gambit
stories.text:
Corvhen had stolen bones for five years before she understood what she was stealing: history. Each skeleton in the Catacombs Beneath Anthrim held a voice from before the Remaking—a frequency the Dominion wanted silenced. The State offered gold for every set of pre-Ascension remains delivered to the furnace-houses. Corvhen took the work because hunger speaks louder than ethics. But the last skeleton was different. When she lifted it from its niche, it sang. Not with sound—with memory. Corvhen saw a city underwater, towers of living coral, a woman carving bone-syllables into stone. She saw the moment the Remaking shattered the world. She saw a name: *Elara, Boundary-Keeper, sworn to preserve.*

The skeleton gripped her wrist with impossible strength. Corvhen fled the catacombs with the bones wrapped in stolen cloth. She smuggled them through the Shadow-Market beneath Anthrim, past three checkpoints, to a waystation library north of Pelimor where bone-singers kept the old songs alive. When she placed the skeleton on the stone, the singing stopped. Naela, the eldest, examined the bones with trembling hands.

stories.title: The Merchant's Threshold
stories.text:
Torven had spent forty years crossing the Scorched Reach, watching spice caravans vanish between dunes. Now, at the Wailing Scar—where three timelines bled into one market—he understood why. The Scar existed in all three versions of the world simultaneously. A merchant could buy from the past, sell to the future, and keep the present's profit. It was economically perfect. It was also impossible to navigate without losing your name. Torven carried one cargo: a sealed flask of pre-Remaking water, entrusted to him by the Seventeen Transformed Merchants who waited in the Drowned Cathedrals. The water was an anchor. Whoever drank it would remember which timeline they belonged to. He found the counter where reality bent worst. A merchant-woman made entirely of light offered him an impossible price: "Drink your water. Join us. Transcend."

"No," Torven said. He unstoppered the flask and poured the water onto the sand. It pooled, and in its reflection, he saw three versions of himself—one aged, one young, one scattered across starlight.

stories.title: The Locksmith's Daughter
stories.text:
Thom Bronzelock's daughter, Kira, inherited his gift for mechanisms—but not his oath to the Dominion. When the Auditors demanded he craft a Truth-Lock that would identify false testimony among refugees, Thom complied. The lock worked perfectly. For a week, it condemned innocents by the dozen. Kira watched her father's hands shake as he forged. She'd learned his craft in secret, the old way—not for control, but for liberation. She broke into the Archive Tower's deepest vault and stole the Truth-Lock before it could be copied. The Dominion's response was swift: they sealed the Lampwick Quarter, imprisoned everyone associated with the Quarter's locksmiths, and announced that the lock's creator—Thom—would be executed at dawn unless the lock was returned. Kira appeared in the Archive Tower's administrative heart. "I have a counteroffer," she told the Chief Auditor. "I will give you a new lock—one that opens any mechanism the Dominion has ever forged."

The Auditor's eyes gleamed with greed. "But," Kira continued, "the lock works only once.

stories.title: The Glassblower's Daughter
stories.text: Messa's mother went into the kiln during the Remaking's first week, caught between furnace-songs and shattering glass. She emerged transformed—partly flesh, partly luminous. The Dominion wanted her for their experiments. Messa stole a consciousness-fragment from Keshtir's vault and fled into the Wailing Scar's three-fold market, where her mother could exist in all versions simultaneously. The auditors followed, but in the fractured market, past and future collapsed. Messa's mother chose to stay in the in-between, where she could see her daughter across all timelines, watching her survive. Some mothers can only love from the margins of broken worlds.
